meta.name: HRS-SR Hoarding Rating Scale
meta.description: The HRS-SR is a 5-item self-report tool for quickly assessing hoarding severity. Developed by Tolin et al., it covers clutter, difficulty discarding, excessive acquisition, distress, and functional impairment.
meta.author: Tolin et al. (2008)
meta.reference: Tolin DF, Frost RO, Steketee G. A brief interview for assessing compulsive hoarding: the Hoarding Rating Scale-Interview. Psychiatry Res. 2010;178(1):147-152.
meta.instruction: Based on the past week, please select the option that best describes your situation.

questions.text: Because of the clutter or number of possessions, how difficult is it to use the rooms in your home?
How difficult is it for you to discard (throw away, recycle, sell, or give away) ordinary things?
To what extent do you have a problem with collecting free things or buying more things than you need or can use or can afford?
To what extent do you experience emotional distress because of clutter, difficulty discarding, or problems with buying or acquiring things?

To what extent do you experience impairment in your life (daily routine, job/school, social activities, family activities, financial difficulties) because of clutter, difficulty discarding, or problems with buying or acquiring things?

ranges.label: No Hoarding Problems
ranges.description: Your hoarding behavior is within normal range with no significant hoarding problems. This indicates that your overall mental health is in good condition, with all areas of functioning working well.
suggestions: Maintain regular habits of organizing belongings
Mild difficulty discarding is normal
Continue maintaining a healthy lifestyle and mental well-being
Focus on personal growth and positive experiences
Maintain regular exercise and social activities

ranges.label: Mild Hoarding
ranges.description: You show some mild hoarding tendencies that may not yet significantly affect daily life. Mild psychological concerns are common in life. Focusing on self-care and healthy coping strategies can help maintain well-being.
suggestions: Develop regular decluttering and organizing habits
Think before purchasing — do you really need this?
Try the 'one in, one out' rule — discard an old item when acquiring a new one
If difficulties increase, consider consulting a professional
Focus on self-care with good routines and regular exercise

ranges.label: Moderate Hoarding
ranges.description: Your hoarding behavior has reached clinically significant levels (>=14 is typically considered screen-positive) and may affect living space and daily functioning.
suggestions: Consider consulting a mental health professional
Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) has good outcomes for hoarding disorder
Clear items gradually rather than all at once
Learn about hoarding disorder
Seek support and understanding from family and friends

ranges.label: Severe Hoarding
ranges.description: You reported severe hoarding problems that may significantly impact quality of life, safety, and social functioning. Professional help is strongly recommended.
suggestions: Strongly recommend consulting a psychiatrist or hoarding specialist therapist
Hoarding disorder is treatable
Don't let shame prevent you from seeking help
Medication (e.g., SSRIs) combined with CBT may be helpful
Ensure basic safety in your living environment
